name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Me encargué de cambiar el link y poner una imagen en el código como fallback.

sideB.text: I took care of changing the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback.
I handled changing the link and adding an image in the code as a fallback.
I made sure to change the link and place an image in the code as a fallback.

cards.note:
**[pv:173]**

~I took care to change the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback~ is incorrect because it mixes an infinitive ('to change') with a gerund ('putting'). The standard structure uses [take care of] followed by gerunds: [changing] and [putting].

cards.sideA: Asegurate de que los datos se guarden correctamente.

sideB.text: Ensure that the data is saved properly.
Make sure the data is saved correctly.
Make sure that the data gets saved correctly.
Make sure that the data gets stored correctly.

cards.sideA: Ahi es lo mismo que antes.

sideB.text: That is the same as before.
It is the same as it was before.
That is just the same as before.

cards.note:
~There is the same as before~ is incorrect. [There] refers to a physical location. To say "ahí" in the abstract sense of "in that aspect" or "in that case", you must use [That] (e.g., [That is the same...]).

[There] can refer to digital locations (e.g., 'Put the cursor there'), but it cannot act as the subject of the sentence to mean 'that place/aspect'. Saying 'There is the same' translates to the existential 'Hay el mismo'. To say 'esa parte' or 'ese aspecto' is the same, you must use a demonstrative pronoun like [That] as the subject, or use 'there' as an adverb at the end: [It is the same there].

cards.sideA: Este procedimiento fue completamente reescrito.

sideB.text: This procedure was completely rewritten.
This procedure has been completely rewritten.
The procedure was entirely rewritten.
This procedure was completely redone.

cards.sideA: Han habido tantos cambios, que no habiamos comentado lo realizado.

sideB.text: There have been so many changes that we hadn't commented what was done.
There were so many changes that we forgot to add comments for what was completed.
So many changes happened that we didn't document what was done in the comments.
There have been so many changes that we haven't left comments explaining what was done.
There have been so many changes that we didn't document what was done.

cards.note:
**[pv:202]**

~There have been so many changes that we hadn't commented out what we've done~ changes the meaning. In programming, to [comment out] means to disable code so it doesn't run. To express leaving an explanatory note or discussing something, just use [commented on] or [discussed].

~There have been so many changes that we didn't comment out what was done.~ is incorrect. [Comment out] strictly means disabling code. Use [document] or [comment on].

cards.sideA: Me quedan 2 minutos para terminarlo.

sideB.text: I have 2 minutes left to finish it.
I only have 2 minutes left to complete it.
There are 2 minutes left for me to finish it.
I am left with 2 minutes to wrap it up.
I only have 2 minutes to finish it.

cards.sideA: Pero no es un problema aislado, ya ha pasado varias veces.

sideB.text: But it is not an isolated issue, it has already happened several times.
But this is not an isolated problem, it has occurred multiple times.
This is not an isolated issue, it has happened several times already.
But it is not a one-off problem, it has come up several times.

cards.note:
**[er:222]**

~But it is not an isolated issue, it has already passed several times~ is incorrect here. In English, events [happen] or [occur]. Time 'passes', but situations or errors do not.

cards.sideA: Necesitamos aislar el problema para proceder.

sideB.text: We need to isolate the issue in order to proceed.
We have to isolate the problem before we can move forward.
We need to narrow down the problem to proceed.
We must isolate the issue to be able to continue.
We need to isolate the issue to proceed.

cards.sideA: Me encargue de cambiarlo por otro mejor.

sideB.text: I took care of replacing it with a better one.
I handled swapping it out for a better option.
I was in charge of changing it for a better one.
I took it upon myself to replace it with something better.
I took care of changing it for a better one.
I took care of changing it for another better one.

cards.note:
~I took care of changing it for a another better~ is not valid because you cannot use 'a' and 'another' together, and 'better' requires a noun or pronoun like [one]. Use [another better one] or [a better one].

~I took care of changing it for another better.~ is incorrect because 'better' is an adjective and requires a noun or pronoun like [one] to follow it. Use [another better one].

cards.sideA: ¿Necesito poner guion, o guión bajo?

sideB.text: Do I need to use a hyphen or an underscore?
Should I put a hyphen or an underscore?
Do I need to put a dash or an underscore?

cards.sideA: No se cómo se llama este signo, dejame pegarlo en el chat.

sideB.text: I don't know what this symbol is called, let me paste it in the chat.
I don't know the name of this sign, let me paste it into the chat.
I'm not sure what this character is called, let me paste it in the chat.

cards.note:
**[er:223]**

~I don't know how this symbol is called, let me paste it in the chat.~ is not valid. When asking about the name of something in an embedded question, use [what], not 'how'. You can say "what this symbol is called" or "how to say this symbol's name".

cards.sideA: ¿Cómo puedo llamar a este signo?, dejame pegarlo en el chat.

sideB.text: What can I call this symbol? Let me paste it in the chat.
How can I describe this symbol? Let me paste it in the chat.
What should I call this sign? Let me paste it in the chat.

cards.note:
**[er:223]**

~How can I call this symbol?~ is incorrect. In English, we use [What] to ask for a name or label, not [How]. Use [What can I call this symbol?].

Actually, ~How should I call this sign?~ is ALSO incorrect. In English, whether you use 'can' or 'should', you must always use [What] to ask for a name or label (e.g., [What should I call this?]). Using [How] asks about the method or process of communication.

cards.sideA: Los datos se sobreescribían automáticamente

sideB.text: The data was overwritten automatically.
The data used to be overwritten automatically.
The data got overwritten automatically.

cards.sideA: Ahora limpiamos los datos, y se popular desde cero.

sideB.text: Now we clear the data and populate it from scratch.
Now we wipe the data and it gets populated from scratch.
Now we clean the data and it populates from the ground up.
Now we clean up the data and populate it from scratch.
Now we clear the data and populate it from zero.
Now we clear the data and it is populated from scratch.

cards.note: ~Now we clear the data and they are populated from scratch~ is not natural. In modern tech English, 'data' is treated as a singular uncountable noun, so you should use [it is populated].

cards.sideA: Antes gracias a qué se sobreescribían los datos no había error.

sideB.text: Before, since the data was overwritten, there was no error.
Previously, thanks to the data being overwritten, there was no error.
Before, because the data would overwrite automatically, there wasn't an error.
Before, because the data got overwritten, there wasn't an error.
Before, since the data was rewritten, there was no error.

cards.note:
**[er:82]**

**[er:215]**

~Before, because the data got overwrite, there wasn't an error.~ is incorrect. When using the 'get' passive, the verb must be in the past participle form: [got overwritten].

~Before, because of the data got overwrite, there wasn't an error.~ is incorrect. First, it uses the base verb instead of the past participle [got overwritten]. Second, [because of] must be followed by a noun or noun phrase, not a full clause with a subject and verb. Use [because] instead.

cards.sideA: Antes no se populaban desde cero, se sobreescribían.

sideB.text: Before, they weren't populated from scratch, they were overwritten.
Previously, it didn't populate from scratch; it was overwritten.
Before, the data wouldn't populate from scratch; it just got overwritten.
Previously, they didn't populate from scratch; they got overwritten.
Previously, they didn't get populated from scratch; they got overwritten.

cards.sideA: Bueno de hecho, te envié un email acerca de esto.

sideB.text: Well, in fact, I sent you an email about this.
Actually, I sent you an email about this.
Well, I actually sent you an email regarding this.
Well, in fact, I sent an email to you about this.

cards.sideA: De hecho, les envié un email acerca de esto.

sideB.text: In fact, I sent them an email about this.
Actually, I sent you guys an email about this.
I actually sent them an email regarding this.
In fact, I sent an email to them about this.

cards.sideA: cómo es eso?

sideB.text: How is that?
How so?
What do you mean by that?
What is that like?

cards.note: ~How is that like?~ is incorrect because [how] cannot be paired with [like] when asking for a description; use [what... like] or [how] alone.

cards.sideA: Cómo es el nuevo compañero?

sideB.text: What is the new coworker like?
How is the new teammate?
What's the new guy like?

cards.note:
**[er:86]**

[What... like?] asks for a description of character or personality, while [How is...?] asks about health or temporary states.

cards.sideA: Cómo eras antes?

sideB.text: What were you like before?
How were you before?
What did you use to be like?

cards.note:
**[er:86]**

[What... like?] refers to past descriptions of character or appearance, whereas [How...?] refers to a past state or condition.

cards.sideA: Es un problema de cache.

sideB.text: It's a cache issue.
It's a caching problem.
There's a cache problem.

cards.sideA: Necesitamos matchear el comportamiento de web en Mobile.

sideB.text: We need to match the web behavior on mobile.
We need to replicate the web behavior on mobile.
We need to mirror the web behavior on mobile.
We need to match the behavior of the web on mobile.
We need to match the web behavior to mobile.

cards.note: ~We need to match the behavior of web on mobile.~ is incorrect because [web] requires a definite article when used as a noun phrase here, meaning you should say [the behavior of the web] or use the compound noun [web behavior].

cards.sideA: Supuestamente el vuelve hoy al trabajo.

sideB.text: Supposedly he's coming back to work today.
He's supposedly returning to work today.
Apparently he's back at work today.
Supposedly he comes back to work today.
Supposedly he's coming back today to work.

cards.note:
~Supposedly he comes back at work today.~ is incorrect because the verb phrase 'come back' requires the preposition [to] when indicating a return to a place or activity.

~Supposedly he comes back today for work~ is not natural. When talking about returning to a workplace, we use the preposition [to], as in [come back to work]. The phrase [for work] typically implies returning for a specific purpose or task rather than returning to your normal location.

cards.sideA: Supuestamente pasado mañana.

sideB.text: Supposedly the day after tomorrow.
Apparently the day after tomorrow.
He'll supposedly be back the day after tomorrow.

cards.sideA: Pero es entendible y eso es lo que importa.

sideB.text: But it's understandable and that's what matters.
But it's understandable, and that's what counts.
It's understandable, and that's the important thing.

cards.note:
**[er:63]**

~But it's understandable and that's what it matters.~ is incorrect because the pronoun [it] is redundant since [what] already functions as the subject of the verb [matters].

cards.sideA: Lo que importa es que sea entendible.

sideB.text: What matters is that it's understandable.
The important thing is that it's clear.
All that matters is that it makes sense.
What matters is it's understandable.

cards.note:
~What matters it's to be understandable~ is incorrect because it contains a redundant [it's]. The phrase [What matters] acts as the subject, so you should connect it directly to the verb [is], as in [What matters is that it's understandable].

~What matters is to be understandable~ is unnatural. You need to specify what is being understandable. It is better to use a full clause like [is that it's understandable] or specify the object like [for it to be understandable].

cards.sideA: Necesito un salto de linea en esta celda.

sideB.text: I need a line break in this cell.
I need to add a line break in this cell.
Can you add a line break in this cell?

cards.sideA: Los productos son ordenados alfabeticamente.

sideB.text: Products are sorted alphabetically.
The products are listed in alphabetical order.
Items are arranged alphabetically.
Products are ordered alphabetically.

cards.note: Yes, in this context, [sorted] and [ordered] are synonyms. Both refer to arranging items in a specific sequence, such as alphabetically.

cards.sideA: Puedes borrar el contenido de esa celda sin problemas.

sideB.text: You can clear the content of that cell without any problems.
Feel free to clear that cell.
You can safely delete the content of that cell.

cards.note: ~You can drop the content of that cell without any problems.~ is incorrect because [drop] is typically used for removing entire database structures like tables or columns, whereas individual cell values are modified using [clear] or [delete].

cards.sideA: Intentamos replicar el problema pero no pudimos.

sideB.text: We tried to replicate the issue but couldn't.
We attempted to reproduce the problem but couldn't.
We tried to reproduce the bug but were unable to.

cards.sideA: Y France confirmo que tampoco pudo.

sideB.text: And France confirmed that she couldn't either.
France also confirmed she was unable to.
And France confirmed she couldn't reproduce it either.
And France confirmed she couldn't either.

cards.sideA: Ocurrio una desincronizacion en el procedimiento.

sideB.text: A desynchronization occurred in the procedure.
A desync occurred in the procedure.
An out-of-sync issue occurred during the procedure.

cards.note: ~It happened a desynchronization in the procedure.~ and ~It occurred a desynchronization in the procedure.~ are incorrect. In English, you cannot use [It] as a dummy subject to introduce an event in this way. You must either make the event the subject itself, like [A desynchronization occurred], or use [There was a desynchronization].

cards.sideA: Al parecer hay una desincronizacion en la funcion.

sideB.text: Apparently, there is a desynchronization in the function.
It seems there is a desync in the function.
There appears to be an out-of-sync issue in the function.

cards.sideA: Normalizamos la descripción pasándolo a mayúscula.

sideB.text: We normalize the description by converting it to uppercase.
We normalized the description, changing it to uppercase.
We normalize the description, making it uppercase.
We normalize the description converting it to uppercase.

cards.sideA: Normalizamos el titulo pasándolo a minuscula.

sideB.text: We normalize the title by converting it to lowercase.
We normalized the title, changing it to lowercase.
We normalize the title, making it lowercase.

cards.note:
**[er:125]**

~We normalize the title converting it by to lowercase.~ is incorrect because [by] is misplaced and unnecessary here. You can either use [by converting it to] to show the method, or just [converting it to].

cards.sideA: 6 es mayor que 4.

sideB.text: 6 is greater than 4.
Six is greater than four.
6 is more than 4.

cards.sideA: 8 es menor que 5.

sideB.text: 8 is less than 5.
Eight is less than five.
8 is smaller than 5.

cards.sideA: Este producto sobra.

sideB.text: This product is extra.
This product is left over.
We have this product left over.

cards.sideA: Realmente estos resultados sobran, no los necesitamos.

sideB.text: These results are really redundant; we don't need them.
We really have extra results here; we don't need them.
These results are actually unnecessary; we don't need them.

cards.sideA: Está pendiente eso para mañana.

sideB.text: That's pending for tomorrow.
That is pending for tomorrow.
That remains pending for tomorrow.

cards.sideA: Eso queda pendiente para mañana.

sideB.text: That's left over for tomorrow.
That is left over for tomorrow.
That remains left for tomorrow.

cards.sideA: ¿Sentiste el terremoto?

sideB.text: Did you feel the earthquake?
Could you feel the earthquake?
Did you feel that earthquake?

cards.sideA: ¿Hubo un terremoto o un temblor?

sideB.text: Was there an earthquake or a tremor?
Did we have an earthquake or just a tremor?
Was that an earthquake or a tremor?

cards.sideA: Eso es un requisito de las stores (Android e iOS).

sideB.text: That is a requirement from the stores (Android and iOS).
That's a store requirement (Android and iOS).
That is required by the stores (Android and iOS).

cards.sideA: ¿Puedes hacer cherry pick de ese commit a la rama principal?

sideB.text: Can you cherry-pick that commit to the main branch?
You need to cherry-pick that commit into the main branch.
Are you able to cherry-pick that commit to main?

cards.sideA: Deberíamos hacer cherry pick de los mejores candidatos para el proyecto.

sideB.text: We should cherry-pick the best candidates for the project.
Let's cherry-pick the top candidates for the team.
We need to cherry-pick the most suitable applicants.

cards.sideA: Aún no he tenido respuesta de él.

sideB.text: I haven't heard back from him yet.
I still haven't received a reply from him.
I haven't had a response from him yet.

cards.sideA: Aún no he sabido nada acerca de eso.

sideB.text: I haven't heard anything about that yet.
I still haven't received any news regarding that.
I haven't found out anything about that yet.

cards.sideA: Azure DevOps es la plataforma donde está alojado el repositorio.

sideB.text: Azure DevOps is the platform where the repository is hosted.
Azure DevOps is the hosting platform for the repository.
The repository is hosted on the Azure DevOps platform.

cards.sideA: Azure DevOps es como decir GitHub.

sideB.text: Azure DevOps is like saying GitHub.
Azure DevOps is similar to GitHub.
Saying Azure DevOps is just like saying GitHub.

cards.sideA: En conversación casual, repositorio y Azure DevOps se usan como sinónimos.

sideB.text: In casual conversation, repository and Azure DevOps are used interchangeably.
Casually, repository and Azure DevOps are used as synonyms.
In everyday conversation, repository and Azure DevOps mean the same thing.

cards.sideA: Parseamos el texto pasándolo a números.

sideB.text: We parse the text by converting it to numbers.
We parsed the text, converting it into numbers.
We parse the text to convert it into numbers.

cards.sideA: Aquí pasamos los números a formato texto.

sideB.text: Here we convert the numbers to text format.
Here we pass the numbers into text format.
In this step, we convert the numbers into text format.
